subject: ap-government
title: SCOTUS Comparison — Cohen v. California (1971)
stimulus:
In 1968 Paul Cohen wore a jacket displaying an obscene anti-war statement while entering a Los Angeles courthouse. Cohen was arrested and charged under a California statute that prohibited "maliciously and willfully disturb[ing] the peace and quiet of any neighborhood or person [by] offensive conduct." Cohen asserted that he wore the jacket in protest of the Vietnam War. Cohen was convicted in a Los Angeles court and sentenced to 30 days in jail.

In the subsequent case Cohen v. California (1971), the Court ruled in a 5–4 decision in favor of Cohen, overturning his conviction. In the majority opinion, Justice John Marshall Harlan rejected the argument that the state could restrict the words on Cohen's jacket as offensive conduct. Harlan reasoned that "[O]ne man's vulgarity is another's lyric," warning that "government might soon seize upon the censorship of particular words as a convenient guise for banning the expression of unpopular views."

parts.prompt: Identify the civil liberty that is common to both Schenck v. United States (1919) and Cohen v. California (1971).
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Identifies freedom of speech as the civil liberty common to both cases.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept 'freedom of speech,' 'free speech,' or 'First Amendment speech rights.' Only freedom of speech earns the point.

parts.prompt: Explain how the facts in Schenck v. United States and Cohen v. California resulted in different holdings.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (first point): Describes relevant information about the required Supreme Court case Schenck v. United States. Accept: Schenck was found guilty of violating the Espionage Act; a man was arrested for distributing anti-war pamphlets that called for people to disobey the draft; the Supreme Court held that Congress has the power to prevent dangerous speech; the First Amendment does not protect speech that presents a clear and present danger to public welfare.

2 pts (second point): Correctly explains how the facts of both cases resulted in different holdings. Accept: the speech in Schenck was a threat (clear and present danger), so the Court held it was not protected, while the speech in Cohen was offensive but did not pose a security threat, so the Court held it was protected; both cases involved speech in protest of a war, but the Court held in Cohen that the government could not limit offensive speech whereas in Schenck it held that speech presenting a clear and present danger can be limited.
parts.scoring_notes: First point requires accurate description of Schenck. Second point requires connecting the factual differences between both cases (threatening/dangerous speech vs. merely offensive speech) to explain the different outcomes.

parts.prompt: Explain how the decision in Cohen v. California reflects the democratic ideal of limited government.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Explains the connection between Cohen and limited government. Accept: the decision in Cohen protects an individual's right to wear a jacket with offensive speech, which constrains the government's power to censor speech; the decision upheld civil liberties meant to protect against government overreach — the government cannot restrict merely offensive expression.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain the connection between the Cohen ruling and limited government — specifically that the ruling constrains governmental power to censor expression deemed offensive.
